_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against SOSORT 2016 guidelines (Negrini 2018 PMID 29435499), BrAIST trial (Weinstein NEJM 2013 PMID 24047455), Cheng AIS Nature Reviews Disease Primers 2015 (PMID 27188385), Weinstein Lancet 2008 review (PMID 18456103), USPSTF 2018 statement (PMID 29318284), USPSTF evidence report (PMID 29318283), Schwab adult scoliosis prevalence Spine 2005 (PMID 15864163). Cobb angle definitions and bracing thresholds per SRS position statement. SRS, AAOS OrthoInfo, NIH NIAMS URLs all verified to return 200.

h1Title: Scoliosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Scoliosis is a three-dimensional deformity of the spine in which the vertebrae rotate and curve sideways more than 10 degrees, measured by the Cobb method on a standing radiograph. It is the most common spinal deformity in children, with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis affecting 2-3% of children aged 10-16; degenerative scoliosis is far more prevalent in older adults, present in roughly 30-60% of people over 60 on imaging surveys. Most curves are mild and never progress, but a minority grow during the adolescent growth spurt or, in adults, with disc and facet wear. Treatment depends on curve size, skeletal maturity, and progression risk, and ranges from observation, to bracing during growth, to posterior spinal fusion for curves above 45-50 degrees.

keyStats.prevalence: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis 2-3% of children 10-16; adult degenerative scoliosis 30-60% of adults over 60 on imaging
keyStats.demographics: Girls 5-10x more likely than boys to develop a progressive AIS curve over 30 degrees
keyStats.avgAge: AIS detected age 10-16; congenital scoliosis usually before age 5; degenerative scoliosis after age 50
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 25-30 million children worldwide with AIS; tens of millions of adults with degenerative scoliosis

definition: Scoliosis (ICD-10: M41) is a structural lateral curvature of the spine of 10 degrees or more on a standing posteroanterior radiograph, measured by the Cobb method, almost always accompanied by vertebral rotation that produces a rib hump or lumbar prominence on forward bending. It is classified by age of onset (infantile under age 3, juvenile 3-9, adolescent 10-17, and adult after skeletal maturity) and by cause: idiopathic (about 80% of pediatric cases, no identifiable cause), congenital (vertebral malformation present at birth), neuromuscular (cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, spinal muscular atrophy, spinal cord injury), syndromic (Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, neurofibromatosis), and degenerative or de novo scoliosis arising in adulthood from asymmetric disc and facet wear. The Lenke classification subdivides surgical AIS curves into six types based on apical location, structural compensatory curves, lumbar modifier, and sagittal alignment. Severity is graded by Cobb angle: 10-25 degrees mild, 25-45 moderate, above 45 severe. Most patients are managed by pediatric or adult spine orthopedic surgeons, with neurology, genetics, pulmonology, and rehabilitation involved depending on etiology and severity.

typesClassification.description: By far the most common form, presenting age 10-17 with no identifiable underlying cause. Right thoracic curves predominate. Girls are 5-10x more likely than boys to develop curves over 30 degrees that need treatment.

Onset between ages 3 and 9, accounting for 10-15% of pediatric idiopathic curves. Higher risk of progression and pulmonary compromise than AIS, and a workup for underlying neural axis abnormalities with MRI is standard.
Onset before age 3. Most curves under 20 degrees with a rib-vertebral angle difference under 20 degrees resolve spontaneously; larger curves progress and need serial casting or bracing.

Caused by malformed vertebrae from disordered embryologic spine development — hemivertebra, wedge vertebra, or unsegmented bar. About 60% progress; associated cardiac, renal, and spinal cord anomalies should be screened with echocardiogram, renal ultrasound, and spine MRI.

Develops in children with cerebral palsy, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, spinal muscular atrophy, myelomeningocele, or spinal cord injury. Long C-shaped curves involving the pelvis are typical; progression continues into adulthood and often impairs sitting balance and pulmonary function.

Part of a generalized connective tissue or neurocutaneous disorder such as Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, or neurofibromatosis type 1. Curves are often atypical (left thoracic), early-onset, and require genetics-aware surgical planning due to dural ectasia and vertebral fragility.
Untreated AIS persisting into adulthood. Symptoms often emerge in the 40s-60s as the curve degenerates further with disc and facet wear superimposed on the existing deformity.

Develops after age 50 from asymmetric disc and facet degeneration, almost always in the lumbar spine. Imaging surveys find lumbar curves over 10 degrees in 30-60% of adults over 60. Pain, radiculopathy, and neurogenic claudication are the dominant symptoms rather than the deformity itself.

primarySymptoms: Visible asymmetry of the back when bending forward — one side of the rib cage or lumbar paraspinal muscles is more prominent than the other (the rib hump seen on the Adams forward-bend test).
Uneven shoulders, with one shoulder blade appearing higher or more prominent than the other, often first noticed by a parent, school nurse, or swim coach.
Asymmetric waist creases or a tilted pelvis, with clothing hanging unevenly and one hip looking higher than the other.
Head not centered over the pelvis when viewed from behind, producing a leaning posture that worsens as the curve grows.
Back pain, particularly in adult degenerative scoliosis — present in 60-80% of adults with de novo curves, located in the lumbar region and aggravated by standing or walking.
Leg pain, numbness, or weakness from foraminal narrowing in degenerative scoliosis — radicular symptoms can dominate over axial back pain.
Neurogenic claudication in adult lumbar scoliosis — pain and heaviness in the legs after walking a defined distance, relieved by sitting or leaning forward.
Shortness of breath and reduced exercise tolerance in severe thoracic curves above 70-80 degrees, where rib cage rotation compromises lung volumes.
Difficulty sitting upright in children with neuromuscular scoliosis, with progressive loss of trunk control and pressure points where the rib cage meets the pelvis.
Skin changes such as café-au-lait spots, hair patches, dimples, or sinuses overlying the spine — red flags that point to a syndromic or congenital underlying cause.
earlyWarningSigns: Asymmetry first noticed at a routine school or pediatric screening using the Adams forward-bend test, often before any symptoms
A scoliometer reading of 7 degrees or more of trunk rotation on forward bending — the standard threshold for radiographic referral
Rapid changes in clothes fit or posture during the adolescent growth spurt, especially in girls around age 10-13
A family history of scoliosis in a parent or sibling raises a child's risk roughly 3-7 fold
New, persistent back pain in an adolescent — most AIS is painless, so significant pain warrants imaging to exclude tumor, infection, or spondylolysis
emergencySigns: New leg weakness, foot drop, or loss of bladder or bowel control with back pain — possible spinal cord or cauda equina compression requiring same-day imaging
Severe, escalating back pain in a child with scoliosis that wakes them from sleep or is unresponsive to simple analgesia — exclude tumor, infection, and syrinx with urgent MRI
Sudden worsening of curve appearance with neurologic symptoms — could indicate progression of an underlying syrinx, Chiari malformation, or intraspinal lesion
Acute shortness of breath or chest pain in a patient with a severe thoracic curve — possible cardiopulmonary decompensation, especially in early-onset and neuromuscular scoliosis
Postoperative fever, wound drainage, escalating pain, or new neurologic deficit after spinal fusion — assess immediately for surgical site infection, hematoma, or implant complication

causes.description: Accounts for roughly 80% of pediatric scoliosis cases. Current evidence points to a polygenic susceptibility interacting with growth, neurohormonal factors (low circulating leptin and melatonin patterns), and proprioceptive control during the adolescent growth spurt. No single gene or trigger explains it.

Failure of vertebral formation (hemivertebra, wedge vertebra) or failure of segmentation (unsegmented bar) during the 4th-6th week of embryogenesis produces a structural curve present at birth. Associated cardiac, renal, and intraspinal anomalies occur in 20-40% and need active screening.

In cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, spinal muscular atrophy, and spinal cord injury, asymmetric paraspinal muscle tone and loss of trunk control allow the spine to collapse into a long C-shaped curve. Up to 90% of children with non-ambulatory cerebral palsy develop scoliosis.

Marfan syndrome (fibrillin-1 mutation), Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (collagen defects), and neurofibromatosis type 1 each carry roughly a 30-60% lifetime risk of scoliosis, often with atypical curve patterns and early progression because ligament and bone integrity are abnormal.

causes.cause: Asymmetric disc and facet degeneration (de novo adult scoliosis)
causes.description: After age 50, asymmetric loss of disc height, facet osteoarthritis, and rotatory subluxation gradually produce a new lumbar curve. The dominant symptoms are mechanical low back pain, foraminal stenosis, and neurogenic claudication rather than cosmetic deformity.

causes.cause: Leg length discrepancy and postural compensation
causes.description: True leg length differences over 2 cm produce a compensatory lumbar curve that flexibly corrects on sitting or with a heel lift. This is functional, not structural, and does not show vertebral rotation.

causes.cause: Prior spine surgery, infection, or radiation
causes.description: Childhood radiation to the thoracic or lumbar spine, tuberculous spondylitis, or laminectomy without fusion can create an iatrogenic or post-infective curve through asymmetric growth or destabilization.

riskFactors.description: Girls and boys develop small AIS curves at similar rates, but girls are 5-10x more likely to progress to curves over 30 degrees requiring treatment.
Having a first-degree relative with scoliosis raises personal risk 3-7 fold. Twin studies estimate heritability of AIS at roughly 70-90%.

Most AIS progression occurs in the 12-18 months around peak height velocity, typically age 11-13 in girls and 13-15 in boys. Risser stage 0-2 and open triradiate cartilage predict the highest progression risk.

Risk of progression rises sharply with curve size. Curves under 20 degrees in a growing child have roughly 20% risk of progression; curves over 30 degrees progress in 60-70%; curves over 50 degrees continue to progress into adulthood at about 1 degree per year.
Risser grade 0-1 (open iliac apophyses, premenarchal) combined with a 20-29 degree curve carries a 60-70% progression risk on natural history data; Risser 4-5 carries under 10%.
Cerebral palsy GMFCS levels IV-V, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, and spinal muscular atrophy carry 60-90% lifetime risk of progressive scoliosis.
Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and neurofibromatosis type 1 each carry 30-60% lifetime risk of scoliosis and often progress faster than idiopathic curves.
Hemivertebra, wedge vertebra, and unsegmented bars present at birth carry a 60% progression rate, highest with unilateral unsegmented bars opposite a contralateral hemivertebra.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 50 with lumbar disc and facet degeneration
riskFactors.description: Imaging surveys identify lumbar de novo curves over 10 degrees in 30-60% of adults over 60; the prevalence rises steadily with each decade.
Asymmetric vertebral compression fractures from low bone density can both initiate and accelerate adult de novo scoliosis; treating osteoporosis is part of long-term adult-deformity care.

affectedDemographics: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis affects 2-3% of children aged 10-16 (SRS, USPSTF 2018 evidence review)
Roughly 0.3-0.5% of adolescents have curves over 20 degrees, and 0.1% have curves over 40 degrees requiring surgical evaluation
Girls are 5-10x more likely than boys to develop progressive curves needing brace or surgical treatment
Adult degenerative scoliosis prevalence rises to 30-60% in adults over 60 in imaging-based volunteer cohorts (Schwab 2005 Spine)
Up to 90% of children with non-ambulatory cerebral palsy (GMFCS IV-V) develop scoliosis; nearly all boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy do as they lose ambulation
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of scoliosis combines a structured examination with a single standing posteroanterior radiograph. The Adams forward-bend test is the standard initial screen: the patient bends forward at the waist with arms hanging, and the clinician inspects the back from behind for a unilateral rib or lumbar prominence. A scoliometer placed across the deformity at its apex quantifies trunk rotation; a reading of 7 degrees or more is the conventional threshold for referral for imaging. A full-length standing posteroanterior spine radiograph is the diagnostic gold standard. The Cobb angle is measured by drawing lines along the superior endplate of the most cranially tilted vertebra and the inferior endplate of the most caudally tilted vertebra, then measuring the angle between perpendiculars to these lines; 10 degrees or more confirms scoliosis. Skeletal maturity is assessed simultaneously using the Risser grade of iliac apophyseal ossification (0-5) and the triradiate cartilage; these drive progression risk and treatment thresholds. MRI of the entire neural axis is added in atypical presentations — onset before age 10, left thoracic curves, rapid progression, painful scoliosis, abnormal neurological examination, or congenital and syndromic forms — to look for syrinx, Chiari malformation, tethered cord, or intraspinal tumor, found in 15-25% of such cases. Pulmonary function testing is added in severe thoracic curves and in neuromuscular scoliosis. In adult de novo scoliosis, full-length standing radiographs and a lumbar MRI are standard to characterize curve magnitude, sagittal balance, and any neurogenic component. Routine school screening with the forward-bend test remains controversial: the USPSTF in 2018 concluded that the evidence for and against screening adolescents 10-18 for idiopathic scoliosis is currently insufficient (I statement), while the Scoliosis Research Society, AAOS, and AAP continue to recommend screening at school health visits.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standard clinical screen for scoliosis. Identifies trunk rotation and a rib or lumbar prominence; a scoliometer reading of 7 degrees or more triggers radiographic referral.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient bends forward at the waist with arms relaxed downward and feet together. The clinician inspects from behind and places a small handheld device across the back at the curve apex. Takes 2-5 minutes, no preparation, no radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: Standing posteroanterior and lateral spine radiograph
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis and Cobb angle measurement. Lateral views assess kyphosis, lordosis, and sagittal balance. Standing position is essential — supine films underestimate curve size by 10-20%.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A full-length spine X-ray taken in the standing position with arms relaxed. Takes 10-15 minutes. Modern protocols (EOS imaging, low-dose biplanar) reduce radiation dose to roughly one-tenth of conventional X-rays.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Skeletal maturity marker derived from the same standing radiograph. Grades the degree of iliac apophyseal ossification (0-5), which drives progression risk and brace eligibility.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Read from the standing PA radiograph along with the triradiate cartilage status. No additional imaging required. Reported alongside the Cobb angle in the radiology report.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the spine and brain
diagnosticTests.purpose: Used in atypical scoliosis — early-onset, left thoracic, painful, rapidly progressive, with neurologic findings, or syndromic/congenital — to detect syrinx, Chiari malformation, tethered cord, or intraspinal tumor (15-25% yield in atypical cases).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan with the patient lying still inside a tunnel-shaped machine. No radiation. Younger children may require sedation. Pacemakers and certain implants are contraindications; check beforehand.

diagnosticTests.test: EOS low-dose biplanar imaging or full-length standing radiograph series
diagnosticTests.purpose: Used for surgical planning to assess coronal and sagittal balance, pelvic incidence, sagittal vertical axis, and global alignment in adult deformity and complex pediatric curves.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standing scan with simultaneous frontal and lateral images taking 20-30 seconds, with a radiation dose roughly 10% of conventional full-spine X-rays.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify restrictive lung disease in severe thoracic curves above 70-80 degrees and in neuromuscular scoliosis. Forced vital capacity guides surgical timing and anesthetic risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry and lung volume measurement, taking 30-45 minutes. The patient blows into a mouthpiece in seated position; older children and adults tolerate it well.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used in adult de novo scoliosis and before surgical planning in older patients to assess osteoporosis, which influences implant choice and fusion technique.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute painless scan over the lumbar spine and hip. Radiation dose is very low, comparable to a single day of natural background exposure.

treatmentOverview: Scoliosis treatment is matched to age, curve size, skeletal maturity, etiology, and trajectory. The SOSORT 2016 guidelines and SRS position statements organize pediatric care into three tiers: observation, conservative care, and surgery. Curves under 25 degrees in a growing child are observed with a standing PA radiograph every 6-12 months; treatment escalates if the curve grows by 5 degrees or more between visits. Curves 25-45 degrees in a skeletally immature patient (Risser 0-2) are the indication for full-time bracing. The BrAIST trial (Weinstein et al., NEJM 2013) provided level-1 evidence that bracing prevents progression to surgical threshold: 72% of braced patients reached skeletal maturity with a curve below 50 degrees compared with 48% of those observed, with a dose-response — the more hours of wear per day, the lower the progression rate. Custom thoracolumbosacral orthoses (TLSO; Boston, Wilmington, Cheneau, Rigo-Cheneau) worn 16-23 hours daily are standard; nighttime-only braces (Providence, Charleston) are an alternative for single lumbar or thoracolumbar curves. Physiotherapeutic scoliosis-specific exercises (Schroth, SEAS, FED) are conditionally recommended adjuncts in SOSORT 2016 and may modestly reduce progression in mild curves; they do not replace bracing for moderate curves. Surgery — almost always posterior spinal fusion with pedicle screws and titanium rods — is indicated for curves over 45-50 degrees in growing patients, curves over 50-55 degrees at skeletal maturity, progressive curves despite bracing, neuromuscular curves over 50 degrees impairing sitting balance, and congenital curves with documented progression. Vertebral body tethering, a fusionless growth-modulation technique, was FDA-approved in 2019 for selected skeletally immature patients with curves 30-65 degrees. In adult degenerative scoliosis, the dominant problem is pain and stenosis rather than curve size; first-line care is structured exercise, weight management, NSAIDs, epidural steroid injections, and physiotherapy. Surgery — decompression with or without short or long-segment fusion — is reserved for severe leg pain, neurogenic claudication, or progressive sagittal imbalance unresponsive to conservative care, and carries higher complication rates than pediatric fusion (15-30% complications, 10-15% revision rates within 2 years in published series).

medicalTreatments.name: Active observation with serial radiographs
medicalTreatments.description: Standard for AIS curves under 25 degrees in growing patients and curves under 30 degrees at skeletal maturity. Standing PA radiograph every 6-12 months tracks progression; bracing or surgery is escalated only if the curve grows by 5 degrees or more.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Most curves under 25 degrees do not progress; only about 10% of small AIS curves reach surgical threshold without intervention (Weinstein Lancet 2008).

medicalTreatments.name: Custom thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO; Boston, Cheneau, Rigo-Cheneau brace), 16-23 hours daily
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for AIS curves 25-45 degrees in skeletally immature patients (Risser 0-2). Worn until skeletal maturity, typically 2-4 years. Adherence is monitored with embedded temperature sensors in modern braces.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents progression to the 50-degree surgical threshold in 72% of patients vs 48% with observation (BrAIST trial, Weinstein NEJM 2013); dose-response by hours worn per day.

medicalTreatments.name: Nighttime-bending brace (Providence, Charleston), 8-10 hours overnight
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to full-time TLSO for single thoracolumbar or lumbar curves with apex at L1 or below. Applies a corrective force during sleep only; better tolerated than daytime braces in compliant adolescents.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to full-time TLSO for appropriately selected single lumbar curves; less effective for double major or thoracic curves above T8.

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapeutic scoliosis-specific exercises (Schroth method, SEAS, FED)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured 3D corrective exercise programs taught by certified physiotherapists, typically 1-2 supervised sessions per week with daily home practice. Conditionally recommended in SOSORT 2016 as an adjunct to observation or bracing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in Cobb angle progression in mild curves (average 2-4 degree advantage over generic exercise in RCTs); does not replace bracing for moderate curves.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 220-500 mg twice daily; ibuprofen 400-800 mg three times daily; celecoxib 100-200 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacologic option for the mechanical low back pain of adult degenerative scoliosis. Use the lowest effective dose; co-prescribe a proton pump inhibitor in patients over 65 or with prior peptic ulcer disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful pain relief in 50-60% of patients with mild-to-moderate degenerative scoliosis pain.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physical therapy and exercise program for adult scoliosis
medicalTreatments.description: Combines lumbar stabilization, hip and core strengthening, postural training, and aerobic conditioning. Typically 8-12 weeks of supervised therapy followed by a home program. Strongly recommended as first-line for adult degenerative scoliosis pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 60-70% of adults with degenerative scoliosis at 6 months; reduces NSAID and injection use.

medicalTreatments.name: Epidural steroid injection or selective nerve root block
medicalTreatments.description: Image-guided injection used for radicular leg pain or foraminal stenosis in degenerative scoliosis when oral therapy and physiotherapy have not controlled symptoms. Limited to 3-4 injections per year per level.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Short-term radicular pain relief of 50% or more in roughly 50-60% of patients at 4-12 weeks; benefit beyond 6 months is inconsistent.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (30 mg daily for 1 week, then 60 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor with central pain-modulating effect. Useful for chronic adult scoliosis back pain, especially with co-existing sleep disturbance or low mood. Watch for nausea, dry mouth, and hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40-45% of patients achieve at least 30% pain reduction at 12 weeks.

surgicalOptions.name: Posterior spinal instrumentation and fusion
surgicalOptions.description: Workhorse operation for AIS curves over 45-50 degrees and adult curves causing intractable symptoms. Pedicle screws are placed at multiple levels and connected with two titanium rods; the curve is corrected by rotation and translation, and the fused segments unite over 6-12 months. Lenke classification guides fusion levels.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curve correction of 50-70%, durable into adulthood; major neurologic complications under 1% in modern series; 90-95% patient-reported satisfaction at 2 years in AIS.

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior spinal fusion (thoracoscopic or open)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for selected thoracolumbar or lumbar curves where shorter fusion is desirable, often combined with posterior surgery in larger or rigid curves. Less commonly performed than posterior fusion since the advent of pedicle screws.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable correction to posterior fusion in selected single-curve patterns; higher pulmonary morbidity in open thoracic approaches.

surgicalOptions.name: Vertebral body tethering (VBT)
surgicalOptions.description: Fusionless growth-modulation surgery in which a cord is anchored to the convexity of the vertebral bodies via thoracoscopic screws, allowing the concave side to grow and correct the curve over time. FDA-approved 2019 for skeletally immature patients with curves 30-65 degrees and at least 1 year of growth remaining.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curve correction of 40-50% at 2 years in published series; revision or conversion to fusion rates of 15-25% within 5 years; long-term outcomes still maturing.

surgicalOptions.name: Growing-rod constructs and magnetically controlled growing rods (MAGEC)
surgicalOptions.description: Used in early-onset scoliosis to control the curve while allowing spinal and pulmonary growth. Magnetically controlled rods are lengthened non-invasively in clinic every 3-6 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curve control in 70-85% of patients with preservation of spinal growth; high rate of implant-related complications (up to 50% over the full treatment course) is typical of growing-spine surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Decompression with limited posterior fusion (adult degenerative scoliosis)
surgicalOptions.description: Used in older adults with stenosis-dominant degenerative scoliosis without severe deformity or sagittal imbalance. Aims to relieve radicular pain and neurogenic claudication while limiting fusion morbidity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain and function improvement in 70-80% of carefully selected patients at 2 years; less effective when sagittal vertical axis is markedly positive.

surgicalOptions.name: Long-segment fusion with sagittal-balance correction (adult deformity)
surgicalOptions.description: Posterior pedicle-screw fusion from thoracic to pelvic levels, often with osteotomy, used for adults with severe coronal and sagittal imbalance. Major reconstructive surgery with substantial morbidity profile.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant improvement in pain and quality-of-life scores in 70-80% of patients at 2-5 years; complication rates 15-30% and revision rates 10-15% within 2 years in large registries.

alternativeTreatments.name: Schroth physiotherapy (Schroth Best Practice / Asklepios)
alternativeTreatments.description: Three-dimensional, pattern-specific corrective exercise method developed for scoliosis. SOSORT 2016 conditionally recommends as an adjunct; RCTs show small but reproducible advantages over generic exercise in Cobb angle progression and quality-of-life scores.

alternativeTreatments.name: SEAS (Scientific Exercise Approach to Scoliosis)
alternativeTreatments.description: Outpatient exercise protocol developed by ISICO that pairs self-correction techniques with strengthening. Conditionally recommended in SOSORT 2016; useful when daily access to a Schroth specialist is not feasible.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a pediatric or adult spine orthopedic surgeon when a scoliometer reading is 7 degrees or more, a radiograph shows a Cobb angle of 20 degrees or more, the curve has progressed by 5 degrees between visits, the child is skeletally immature with a curve over 25 degrees, the patient has a syndromic or neuromuscular underlying diagnosis, the curve is atypical (left thoracic, painful, neurologic findings), or symptoms in adult scoliosis are unresponsive to 3 months of structured conservative care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a fellowship-trained pediatric spine surgeon for children and a fellowship-trained adult spinal deformity surgeon for adults; the two practices have meaningfully different techniques and complication profiles. High-volume centers and surgeons with annual deformity case loads over 50 show lower complication and revision rates in published registries. Confirm access to intraoperative neuromonitoring, dedicated pediatric anesthesia, and a multidisciplinary team including physiotherapists trained in Schroth or SEAS for non-operative care. For adult deformity, ask whether the surgeon routinely measures sagittal vertical axis, pelvic incidence, and lumbar lordosis as part of preoperative planning.
preventionStrategies: Have children examined at well-child visits during the adolescent growth spurt — early detection allows bracing while the curve is still under 45 degrees, which prevents most progression to surgery

Pursue a full evaluation, including MRI of the neural axis, for atypical findings (early onset, left thoracic curve, pain, or abnormal neurology) to catch underlying causes that have treatments of their own
Use a scoliometer at the Adams forward-bend test to objectively quantify trunk rotation rather than relying on visual inspection alone
Treat osteoporosis aggressively in adults over 50 — asymmetric vertebral fragility fractures can initiate or accelerate de novo lumbar scoliosis
Maintain core, hip, and paraspinal strength through resistance training across the lifespan to support spinal stability
lifestyleModifications: Wear the prescribed brace for the full prescribed hours per day — hours of wear, not weeks of wear, drive outcomes (BrAIST dose-response)
Continue full participation in sports, dance, and physical education during bracing — restriction is no longer evidence-based and harms quality of life
Practice the prescribed scoliosis-specific exercises daily at home and re-attend in-clinic sessions every 4-6 weeks during the active growth phase
Sleep on a firm mattress with good lumbar support, especially with adult degenerative scoliosis pain
Maintain a healthy body weight — each kilogram of excess weight increases lumbar spine compressive load and worsens adult scoliosis symptoms
Stop smoking before any planned spinal fusion — smoking reduces fusion rates by approximately 20% and roughly doubles wound and infection complications
Use a sit-stand workstation and move every 30-60 minutes if degenerative scoliosis pain worsens with sustained sitting or standing

recommended: Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day from dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, and fortified foods — supports bone health and reduces fragility risk that accelerates adult curves
Vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day depending on serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D — improves bone mineral density and fall prevention
Adequate protein at 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day to preserve paraspinal and core muscle, particularly around any planned spinal surgery
Mediterranean-style eating pattern emphasizing vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, olive oil, and fish — associated with lower systemic inflammation
Hydration of 1.5-2 liters daily unless restricted for other medical reasons
avoid: Sustained ultra-processed and high-sugar diets that drive weight gain and systemic inflammation
Excess alcohol — increases osteoporosis risk and impairs healing after spinal surgery
Crash dieting that loses muscle along with fat; pair gradual weight loss with resistance exercise
Tobacco in all forms — directly impairs spinal fusion and is a contraindication to elective deformity surgery in many practices
Carbonated colas in large daily volumes — observational data link very high intake with lower bone mineral density in women

exerciseGuidelines: Children with scoliosis should continue full participation in sports, dance, and physical education — exercise restriction has no evidence base and worsens self-image during a vulnerable period. Scoliosis-specific corrective exercises (Schroth, SEAS) are added as a daily home practice with weekly clinic check-ins during the active growth phase. Adults with degenerative scoliosis should aim for 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity (walking, cycling, swimming) plus two sessions of resistance training focused on core, hip, and posterior chain. Aquatic exercise is particularly useful when standing tolerance is limited. Avoid heavy axial loading (deadlifts to maximum weight, military press) without supervised coaching in symptomatic adult scoliosis.
dailyManagement: Track brace wear time using the embedded temperature sensor and review the data with the orthotist at each visit — adherence is the strongest predictor of brace success
Inspect the skin under the brace daily for redness, breakdown, or pressure sores, especially over bony prominences
Perform the prescribed scoliosis-specific exercises every day, ideally split into 2-3 short sessions
Photograph posture in standing and forward-bend positions every 3-6 months to track changes between clinic visits
Maintain medication adherence for adult degenerative scoliosis, including NSAIDs, neuropathic pain agents, and osteoporosis treatment
Keep an updated list of medications, prior surgeries, and imaging studies for spine clinic appointments to avoid repeat radiographs
prognosis: Most scoliosis curves are mild and do not affect health or function across a lifetime. Curves that reach skeletal maturity below 30 degrees rarely progress in adulthood and have a near-normal pregnancy, work, and sports profile. Curves between 30 and 50 degrees at skeletal maturity progress by an average of 1 degree per year into adulthood, more rapidly in thoracic curves and in females, and may need surgical assessment in the 40s-60s. Curves above 50 degrees at maturity tend to progress steadily and often require surgical correction at some point. Bracing in skeletally immature patients with curves 25-45 degrees prevents progression to the 50-degree surgical threshold in 72% of patients (BrAIST trial). Posterior spinal fusion delivers 50-70% curve correction with patient-reported satisfaction of 90-95% at 2-5 years in AIS series. In adult degenerative scoliosis, pain and function rather than curve size determine prognosis — most patients respond to structured conservative care; long-segment surgical reconstruction improves quality-of-life scores in 70-80% but carries higher complication and revision rates than pediatric fusion.
recoveryTimeline: Bracing is a multi-year commitment from diagnosis until skeletal maturity (Risser 4-5), typically 2-4 years for an adolescent diagnosed at age 11-13. After posterior spinal fusion for AIS, most patients are walking with assistance within 1-2 days, discharged home in 3-5 days, return to school in 4-6 weeks, resume non-contact sport at 3-6 months, and contact or collision sports at 9-12 months. Fusion mass consolidation continues for 6-12 months. Adult deformity surgery recovery is slower: most patients walk with aids within a week, return to non-physical work at 6-12 weeks, and reach their plateau of functional improvement by 12-18 months.
complications: Curve progression into adulthood with progressive deformity, pain, and reduced quality of life — preventable in most cases through timely bracing or surgery during growth
Cardiopulmonary compromise in severe thoracic curves above 70-80 degrees, with restrictive lung disease, exercise intolerance, and right heart strain in untreated early-onset cases
Persistent or new back pain in adult untreated AIS, present in roughly 60-80% by age 50, often manageable with conservative care
Foraminal stenosis with radiculopathy and neurogenic claudication in degenerative scoliosis, which can dominate the clinical picture in older adults

Surgical complications including blood loss requiring transfusion, surgical site infection (approximately 1-3% in AIS, higher in neuromuscular and adult deformity surgery), implant prominence or breakage, pseudarthrosis (failed fusion), adjacent segment disease, and rare but serious neurologic injury
Psychosocial effects of visible deformity in adolescents — body-image concerns and lower self-esteem are well-documented and should be screened for and addressed alongside the physical curve

supportResources.name: Scoliosis Research Society — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: The international society of scoliosis surgeons; patient-facing pages on diagnosis, bracing, surgery, and living with scoliosis, plus a global surgeon locator.

supportResources.name: AAOS OrthoInfo — Idiopathic Scoliosis in Children and Adolescents
supportResources.description: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons patient-facing summary aligned with current US clinical practice.

supportResources.name: NIH NIAMS — Scoliosis
supportResources.description: US National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases — authoritative federal patient education resource.

supportResources.name: National Scoliosis Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based patient organization offering support groups, educational materials, and a national directory of scoliosis specialists.

confusedWithConditions.name: Kyphosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Kyphosis is excessive forward curvature of the spine in the sagittal plane (round-back), seen on lateral view radiograph, whereas scoliosis is a lateral curvature with rotation in the coronal plane, seen on PA view. The two can coexist as kyphoscoliosis, especially in Scheuermann's disease, neuromuscular conditions, and severe AIS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lordosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lordosis is excessive inward curvature of the lumbar spine on lateral view, again a sagittal-plane deformity. Scoliosis is a coronal-plane curve with vertebral rotation. Adolescents with AIS often have an associated thoracic hypokyphosis or lumbar hyperlordosis, but the dominant deformity is coronal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spondylolisthesis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spondylolisthesis is forward slippage of one vertebra over another, visible on lateral X-ray, often presenting as low back pain and hamstring tightness in adolescents. The spine is straight in the coronal plane, in contrast to scoliosis. The two occasionally coexist in adults.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lumbar stenosis is narrowing of the spinal canal causing neurogenic claudication and leg-dominant symptoms in older adults, often with a straight or only mildly curved spine. Adult degenerative scoliosis can produce identical symptoms but adds a structural lateral curve over 10 degrees on standing PA radiograph; the two frequently coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Kyphosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Sagittal-plane spine deformity that frequently coexists with scoliosis as kyphoscoliosis, especially in Scheuermann's disease and neuromuscular curves.

relatedConditions.name: Spondylolisthesis
relatedConditions.relevance: Vertebral slippage that can occur in adolescents with axial back pain — an important differential when scoliosis is painful, and a common coexisting finding in adult degenerative deformity.

relatedConditions.name: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with adult degenerative scoliosis and drives the leg-dominant symptoms that determine surgical decisions.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is usually painless, but back pain dominates the adult degenerative-scoliosis presentation; investigate painful pediatric scoliosis carefully for an underlying cause.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Asymmetric vertebral fragility fractures can initiate and accelerate adult de novo scoliosis; treating osteoporosis is part of adult-deformity care.

relatedConditions.name: Marfan Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Connective tissue disorder with roughly 60% lifetime risk of scoliosis; atypical curve patterns and dural ectasia require genetics-aware surgical planning.

relatedConditions.name: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Collagen disorder that increases risk of early-onset scoliosis and complicates bracing and surgical fixation through ligamentous laxity and tissue fragility.

relatedConditions.name: Muscular Dystrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: Boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy almost universally develop progressive neuromuscular scoliosis after loss of ambulation; early posterior fusion preserves sitting balance and pulmonary function.

faqs.question: Is scoliosis curable?
faqs.answer: Scoliosis is not curable in the sense that the underlying tendency to curve is structural, but it is highly controllable. Most curves never progress and need no treatment beyond observation. Bracing during growth prevents most moderate curves from reaching surgical threshold, and posterior spinal fusion permanently corrects severe curves with 50-70% reduction and 90-95% patient-reported satisfaction in adolescent series.

faqs.question: How is scoliosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Scoliosis is diagnosed by a standing full-length posteroanterior spine radiograph showing a Cobb angle of 10 degrees or more. The Adams forward-bend test and a handheld scoliometer screen for the rib or lumbar prominence that prompts imaging — a reading of 7 degrees or more typically triggers X-rays. MRI is added when the presentation is atypical (early onset, left thoracic curve, painful, abnormal neurology).

faqs.question: What is a Cobb angle?
faqs.answer: The Cobb angle is the standard measurement of scoliosis curve severity, taken on a standing PA radiograph. Lines are drawn along the superior endplate of the most tilted upper vertebra and the inferior endplate of the most tilted lower vertebra; the angle between perpendiculars to these lines is the Cobb angle. 10 degrees confirms scoliosis; 25-45 is moderate; over 45 is severe.

faqs.question: What causes scoliosis?
faqs.answer: About 80% of pediatric scoliosis is idiopathic — no single identifiable cause, with a strong genetic component (heritability around 70-90%) interacting with growth and neurohormonal factors. The remainder is congenital (malformed vertebrae), neuromuscular (cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy), syndromic (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, neurofibromatosis), or adult degenerative from asymmetric disc and facet wear after age 50.

faqs.question: Does scoliosis run in families?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis has a strong familial pattern, with heritability estimates from twin studies of roughly 70-90%. Having a first-degree relative with scoliosis raises personal risk 3-7 fold. Siblings and children of someone with scoliosis should be examined during their adolescent growth spurt to catch curves while they are still small enough to brace.

faqs.question: At what age does scoliosis usually start?
faqs.answer: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is most often detected between ages 10 and 16, with peak progression around the adolescent growth spurt — typically age 11-13 in girls and 13-15 in boys. Congenital scoliosis is usually identified before age 5. Adult degenerative scoliosis is a separate entity, developing after age 50 from disc and facet wear and rising in prevalence with each decade.

faqs.question: Why is scoliosis more common in girls?
faqs.answer: Girls and boys develop small scoliosis curves at roughly similar rates, but girls are 5-10x more likely to progress to curves over 30 degrees that need treatment. The reason is not fully understood; current evidence implicates the timing of the adolescent growth spurt, hormonal patterns, and sex-linked genetic factors. Boys with progressive curves often have larger curves at presentation and need careful follow-up.

faqs.question: Does scoliosis cause back pain?
faqs.answer: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is usually painless. Significant pain in a child with scoliosis is a red flag and warrants imaging to exclude tumor, infection, syrinx, or spondylolysis. In adult untreated scoliosis, back pain becomes common with age — roughly 60-80% of adults with persistent AIS report some back pain by age 50, usually mild and manageable with conservative care. Adult degenerative scoliosis is typically painful from the outset.

faqs.question: Do back braces actually work for scoliosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, when worn as prescribed in a growing patient with a moderate curve. The BrAIST trial (Weinstein, NEJM 2013) showed that bracing prevented progression to the 50-degree surgical threshold in 72% of treated adolescents versus 48% of those observed, with a clear dose-response by hours of wear per day. Bracing is most effective for curves 25-45 degrees in skeletally immature patients.

faqs.question: How many hours per day should a scoliosis brace be worn?
faqs.answer: Standard prescription is 16-23 hours per day until skeletal maturity. The BrAIST trial showed that patients who wore the brace at least 13 hours daily had the lowest progression rates, with benefit increasing for each additional hour. Nighttime-only braces (Providence, Charleston) are an alternative for single thoracolumbar or lumbar curves worn 8-10 hours overnight.

faqs.question: Can scoliosis be corrected without surgery?
faqs.answer: Mild curves under 25 degrees rarely need treatment and are simply observed. Moderate curves 25-45 degrees in growing patients are usually managed with bracing, which prevents progression to surgical threshold in most patients. Established curves over 50 degrees do not reverse without surgery — bracing and physiotherapy do not straighten a structural curve, they only slow or stop further growth.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for scoliosis?
faqs.answer: Surgery is generally recommended for adolescent curves over 45-50 degrees while still growing, curves over 50-55 degrees at skeletal maturity, curves that progress despite proper bracing, neuromuscular curves over 50 degrees that impair sitting, and adult deformity with severe pain, neurologic symptoms, or progressive sagittal imbalance unresponsive to 3-6 months of conservative care.

faqs.question: What happens during scoliosis surgery?
faqs.answer: The standard operation is posterior spinal fusion. Pedicle screws are placed at multiple levels under image guidance, two titanium rods are contoured to the correction, the curve is corrected by rotation and translation, and bone graft is added so the fused segments unite over 6-12 months. Surgery takes 4-8 hours under general anesthesia with continuous intraoperative neurologic monitoring.

faqs.question: How long is recovery from scoliosis surgery?
faqs.answer: Most adolescent patients walk with assistance within 1-2 days, leave hospital in 3-5 days, return to school in 4-6 weeks, resume non-contact sport at 3-6 months, and return to contact sport at 9-12 months. Adult deformity surgery recovery is slower — most patients return to non-physical work at 6-12 weeks and reach maximal functional improvement by 12-18 months.

faqs.question: Can scoliosis cause breathing problems?
faqs.answer: Severe thoracic curves above 70-80 degrees can compromise lung volumes and produce restrictive lung disease, exercise intolerance, and in untreated early-onset cases, right heart strain. Most curves treated under 50 degrees do not affect breathing. In neuromuscular scoliosis and Duchenne muscular dystrophy, pulmonary function is a major driver of surgical timing.

faqs.question: Can adults develop scoliosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adult de novo (degenerative) scoliosis develops after age 50 from asymmetric disc and facet wear, almost always in the lumbar spine. Imaging surveys find lumbar curves over 10 degrees in 30-60% of adults over 60. Symptoms are dominated by back pain, radicular leg pain, and neurogenic claudication rather than visible deformity, and most patients respond to structured non-operative care.

faqs.question: Should children be screened for scoliosis at school?
faqs.answer: The US Preventive Services Task Force concluded in 2018 that current evidence is insufficient to recommend for or against routine school screening (I statement). The Scoliosis Research Society, American Academy of Pediatrics, and American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons continue to recommend screening at adolescent well-child visits using the Adams forward-bend test and a scoliometer.

faqs.question: Is scoliosis caused by carrying a heavy backpack or bad posture?
faqs.answer: No. Heavy backpacks, slouching, and uneven posture do not cause structural scoliosis. They can produce postural asymmetry that disappears on the forward-bend test or with a lumbar support, but they do not create the vertebral rotation and Cobb angle that define true scoliosis. Sleeping position and sitting habits are similarly not causal.

faqs.question: Can someone with scoliosis play sports?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children and adolescents with scoliosis, including those in braces, should continue full participation in sports, dance, and physical education unless a surgeon has placed an individual restriction. Exercise restriction is not evidence-based and worsens self-image during a vulnerable period. After spinal fusion, most surgeons clear non-contact sport at 3-6 months and contact sport at 9-12 months.

faqs.question: Can I have a normal pregnancy with scoliosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most women with curves below 40 degrees have uncomplicated pregnancies and vaginal deliveries. Spinal anesthesia and epidurals are usually feasible but may take longer to site. Pregnancy does not cause meaningful curve progression in stable adult scoliosis. Women with prior spinal fusion should share their operative imaging with the anesthesia team for planning.

faqs.question: How much does scoliosis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Bracing in the US typically costs USD 5,000-10,000 including the custom orthosis and follow-up visits, with insurance usually covering most of it. Posterior spinal fusion list prices range from USD 75,000 to USD 150,000 with insurance covering most for medically necessary cases. In India and many emerging markets the total package for AIS fusion is USD 5,000-12,000. Conservative care for adult scoliosis is comparatively low-cost.

faqs.question: Does Schroth therapy work for scoliosis?
faqs.answer: Schroth physiotherapy is a structured 3D corrective exercise method that the international SOSORT 2016 guidelines conditionally recommend as an adjunct to observation or bracing. Randomized trials show small but reproducible advantages over generic exercise in Cobb angle progression and quality-of-life scores, on the order of 2-4 degrees. It does not replace bracing for moderate curves or surgery for severe curves.

metaDescription: Scoliosis: sideways spine curve over 10 degrees Cobb angle. Symptoms, Cobb measurement, bracing, surgery, and prognosis reviewed by our medical board.
